data.title: Dutch Bros: Positive Fundamental Change But Still Cautious (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Investment thesis

Previously I wrote about Dutch Bros, Inc. (NYSE:BROS) in an article titled “Dutch Bros: Fundamental Analysis Warrants Caution To Investors At This Valuation” back in April with a sell rating. Since then, there have been two earnings calls with updates from management about the trajectory of the company and the stock has mildly appreciated by about 7%. Now, comparing this to just holding the market, indicated by the S&P 500 index, you would have done better by buying an index focused ETF over this time. However, as we see more volatility with individual holdings, there has been opportunity since the last time I discussed BROS to profit if you’re following a different investment strategy than buying and holding for the long-term. Given my strategy of value investing and looking for great companies at fair prices, I wanted to re-evaluate BROS to see how fundamentals have changed over the last six months.

Given my fundamental analysis below and the current market conditions favoring a risk on, I’m upgrading to a hold rating at this time meaning that I would not favor either buying or selling this company right now. I still believe there is significant risks as highlighted in my previous article, however, some of the fundamentals have improved quarter-over-quarter which is an indication to me that some things may be turning around.

Summary of Last Coverage

In my last article, I discussed the previous fundamental picture of BROS and made a comparison to the early years of an industry peer, Starbucks Corporation (SBUX). At the time, I highlighted how BROS was trading at ~100 times forward GAAP P/E ratio with a significantly negative ratio of return on invested capital compared to weighted cost of capital ("ROIC-WACC") which is one fundamental indicator I consistently evaluate. Additionally, I mentioned how the growth of cost of goods sold was outpacing their growth in revenue. When compared to SBUX early on, we found that they also had a similar ROIC-WACC ratio, but SBUX revenue growth had outpaced cost of goods sold ("COGS"). As mentioned in my last article, part of the headwinds faced by BROS that has impacted their increases in COGS is the higher costs of sugar and the increases in labor costs which was highlighted by the company in one of their previous earnings calls. Now as I’ve added more key fundamentals to my method of evaluation, I wanted to approach BROS with a more in-depth fundamental analysis which changed my view on the company from a sell to a hold rating at this time as I see some key fundamentals positively changing on a quarterly basis.

Earnings Review

Since my last article, there have been two earnings calls, one that occurred in May and another in August. In another month we will also have an update during their November earnings call to see how things have been going in what seems like a consumer-friendly environment. Evaluating the earnings call back in May, the company reported great year-over-year quarterly revenue growth of 39%, sales grew by 24%, and same shop sales also increased by 10% which was the highest quarterly increase that they have seen in this metric. For comparison, their full year same shop sales only increased by 2.8% for the full year in 2023 according to their February earnings call. They also described their expectation to continue opening new stores, with another quarter of over 30 new shop openings. In their August earnings call, the company described consistently high revenue growth of 30% year-over-year, 30+ new shop openings, and same store sales growth of 4.1%.

As you can see just off these earnings highlights, the company is growing revenue at a very high clip right now, but same-store growth is still a concern that I have given that over the previous year it had only been around 2.8%. This is concerning because it means the company will not significantly grow their revenue without taking on substantial debt to open new stores. One of the analysts on August’s call also asked about a timeline for when the company expects to be more self-funding, rather than taking on debt for growth ventures, and a timeline was not provided. The company also continues to highlight the impact of higher wages and how that will continue to be a headwind moving forward which is why they’ve stuck with a more modest guidance. However, as the company mentioned in the May call and continued to discuss in August, I’m excited to see where the mobile order implementation impacts the efficiency of the company as this may be one way to increase revenue per shop and improve same store sales growth in the future, although they did not guide higher with this metric after implementing about 25% of their stores with this.

Quarterly Fundamental Analysis

At the time of this writing, BROS trades at a trailing twelve-month GAAP P/E of 125 and a forward GAAP P/E ratio of 105. BROS also trades at a high trailing twelve-month EV/EBITDA ratio of 26.8, forward EV/EBITDA ratio of 22.7, and a forward price to sales ratio of 3.16. Compared to the sector median, these values are significantly elevated. Forward price to book is also significantly elevated at 10.8. However, as reported in their May earnings call, BROS demonstrated significant revenue growth year over year. When evaluating quarter over quarter, we also see a consistent growth trend with gross margins roughly around 28%. If gross margins improve to over 30% like they were in 2021, this would be a good sign they are managing the additional costs well, but, when comparing to a successful peer company like SBUX they are at the same level.

Margins

As I highlighted above, BROS have maintained their gross margins around 28% over the last few quarters, and this is a level that I would like to see maintained or improved. As you can see in the chart below there are other aspects of their margins that could see some improvement and this still indicates a weak moat in a competitive industry. However, over time, the margins have slightly improved. When looking specifically at free cash flow, it was significantly negative back in December 2021 with a value of over -25%, while in June 2024 it’s only at -1.5%. The increase in mobile orders across all of their shops may be one of the catalysts that helps drive free cash flow which is something I would focus on over the next few quarters. If they can improve free cash flow to a positive, and fund their own growth ventures, this would be a big win for the company. You can also see below that other key margins such as EBITDA and operating margins have also improved since their IPO. If we continue to see this trend, it may present a value-growth opportunity before other investors realize the positive changes in fundamentals.

Return on Invested Capital

One of the points I highlighted in my previous article on BROS was the negative return on investing capital ("ROIC") to weighted cost of capital ("WACC") ratio which I calculate as ROIC-WACC. When searching for great companies, I want higher ROIC than WACC which would be one indicator that management is making smart decisions with the money they are investing into their growth. As you can see, when evaluating quarter-over-quarter, BROS continues to have a negative ROIC-WACC ratio with COGS significantly increasing, however, ROIC has been positive in the last five quarters. In their May earnings call, they mentioned investments into protein coffee and boba which I expect to eventually contribute to higher returns.

Positive Return on Equity

Since my last article, one key fundamental that I have now adopted to evaluate growing companies is return on equity ("ROE"). One example of a high performer that has a very high return on equity in the consumer discretionary sector is Royal Caribbean Cruises Ltd. (RCL) which has been on a tear since I covered the fundamentals back in June. Obviously, there are many factors that go into stock performance of a company, but I’ve come to believe high return on equity is a fundamental that should be significantly considered when evaluating growth companies such as BROS. As you can see in the chart below, BROS is starting to demonstrate positive ROE where it has been significantly negative in the past. Along with positive ROE that is just over 10%, all other key fundamentals have turned positive over the last few months when they have been inconsistent or negative in the past. This could be an indication that the company is turning around some of their operations to produce higher returns for investors and their company rather than needing to continue to use a large amount of debt to artificially produce revenue growth through new store openings which could become an issue in harder economic conditions.

Per Share Value

On a per share basis, we can see that debt continues to remain the highest among the key fundamentals per share. However, in recent quarters they have been able to decrease some of the debt. Overall, they still remain at a negative cash to debt ratio and the company continues to fund growth through large amounts of debt. If we continue in a more favorable, risk on environment, they will likely be okay and turn around these fundamentals. Moreover, we still have negative free cash flow per share and low earnings per share. Over the next few quarters I would focus on if they can become a positive free cash flow company with some of the investments they’ve made such as implementing mobile orders.

Risks to Thesis

As I’m upgrading my previous rating of this company from a sell to a hold, the main risk to this investment thesis is that the underlying fundamentals are not significantly improving enough to where it will correlate to stock returns and produce a good long-term investment. As I have mentioned above, revenue is growing significantly, but so are the costs. Same store sales continue to grow at a low single digit level while ROIC-WACC ratio continues to be negative which both have a drag on BROS’s future profitability and positives that I’ve highlighted in this article. Moreover, this thesis is impacted by the current economic and market conditions which are dynamic and change very quickly. If we enter a more recessionary economic environment, riskier investments, like growth companies with large amounts of debt, are likely to be significantly impacted. Finally, as my thesis is primarily based on historical data and less forward looking expectations, I’m limited in projecting potential growth for this company which readers may see outweighing the current fundamentals.

Conclusion

In conclusion, based on my fundamental analysis above which is beginning to show some improvement in key fundamental metrics quarter-over-quarter, I am upgrading from a sell to a hold rating. I still believe an investment in this company is riskier than some of the other available value investments, which some of these better investments I’ve already covered. One of my main concerns is with the low same store sales growth they previously experienced over the full year, but this may start to be mitigated as mentioned in the most recent two earnings calls. I’m also concerned that there are still some negatives in the aggregate fundamental picture and that BROS historical stock performance has significantly trailed the S&P 500 index while other growth stocks have significantly outperformed. However, with current market conditions favoring higher risk at the moment, the positive change in some of the key fundamentals may act as a catalyst over the short-term. For these reasons, I’m upgrading to a hold rating not favoring either a buy or sell at the moment, but I will be staying away as a long-term value investor until more fundamentals indicate a better picture for my personal risk profile and investing strategy. As management seems to be making good investment decisions into the growth of their business right now, the company is intriguing and should be watched closely if you’re also on the fence. This article is solely based on my own research and opinions and should not be considered financial advice. Investors should perform their own research before making any financial decisions and taking on more speculative companies.

data.title: EPS: Reasonable Valuations, Mixed Performance
data.description:
As much of the good news for the economy and U.S. companies is already priced into the stock market, investors are expected to find greater appeal in investment options still trading at reasonable prices. This strengthens the case for value-oriented funds and similar alternatives, such as the WisdomTree EPS U.S. LargeCap Fund (NYSEARCA:EPS), which mitigates high valuations seen in broader equity indexes by using an earnings-weighted methodology rather than traditional market cap-weighted indexes.

With a price-to-earnings ratio near the 17x level, EPS is still a value play in today’s market. However, a big challenge for EPS and value funds in general is their historical underperformance relative to stock market indexes like the Russell 1000 and the S&P 500. While EPS’s large position in the technology sector is a tailwind for the fund, it remains to be seen whether the fund can outperform going forward.

ETF Description & Highlights

EPS is an exchange-traded fund that offers broad exposure to U.S. large-cap companies with positive earnings, tracking the WisdomTree U.S. LargeCap Index.

This index is composed of the 500 largest companies with positive earnings over the last four quarters. These companies must have a minimum market cap of $100 million and more than $200.000 in daily trading volume over the last six months. Annually, the index is weighted according to the total earnings generated by each constituent so that companies with higher earnings are overweight. There is also a cap of 25% for sector allocation to ensure diversification. This limit drops to 15% for the real estate sector. As the last step, individual weights for each constituent are adjusted according to additional quantitative limits related to market capitalization and trading volume.

As of October 16, 2024, EPS had $1 billion in assets under management, invested in a portfolio of 500 companies with an average market capitalization of $271.7 billion. The fund's market cap allocation is concentrated primarily in mega and large caps, with mega caps representing 43.4% of total assets and large caps accounting for 34.4%. Mid caps make up 21.7% of the portfolio, while small caps represent only 0.5%. Despite being an earnings-weighted fund, EPS's market cap allocation is quite similar to traditional market-cap-weighted indexes like the Russell 1000 and the S&P 500.

EPS’s top ten holdings are Apple (5.45%), Microsoft (5.14%), Alphabet (4.74%), Meta Platforms (3.91%), NVIDIA (3.47%), Amazon (3.04%), JPMorgan Chase (2.75%), Exxon Mobil (2.25%), Berkshire Hathaway (1.82%), and Bank of America (1.58%). Together, these holdings comprise 34% of the portfolio, with a clear predominance of technology and communication services stocks, much like Russell 1000 and S&P 500 indexes.

Below is a table with large-cap ETFs that will serve as the peer group for EPS, using both value and fundamental-based methodologies. The first three funds are traditional value-oriented investment vehicles, starting with the Vanguard Value ETF (VTV), the largest one with $130.67 billion in assets under management, which seeks out undervalued companies following a multifactor approach. Similarly, BlackRock’s iShares Russell 1000 Value ETF (IWD), with $61.18 billion in assets under management, uses on value stocks, derived from the Russell 1000 index, while the iShares S&P 500 Value ETF (IVE) is a subset of the S&P 500 index. The next three funds are the Charles Schwab’s Fundamental U.S. Large Company Index ETF (FNDX) and the VanEck’s MOAT ETF, each following value and fundamentals-driven strategies. Lastly, the Invesco FTSE RAFI 1000 US ETF (PRF) employs its own fundamental approach, focusing on financial metrics to build its portfolio.

From a sector perspective, EPS’s largest allocation is to the technology sector, with 26.5% of total equities, followed by financial services at 17.0%, communication services at 12.7%, healthcare at 10.4%, consumer discretionary at 8.8%, industrials at 7.3%, energy at 6.6%, consumer staples at 5.9%, utilities at 2.2%, basic materials at 1.4%, and real estate at 1.2%. Relative to the Russell 1000 index, EPS is overweight in communication services (+4.0%), financial services (+3.9%), and energy (+3.1%), but underweight in technology (-5.1%), consumer discretionary (-1.5%), and industrials (-1.2%).

This highlights EPS’s bias on sectors and industries generating consistent earnings like telecommunications, driven by holdings in AT&T and Verizon, global banks JPMorgan, Bank of America, and Wells Fargo, and major oil players Exxon Movil and Chevron.

Compared to the peer group, EPS is heavily overweight in technology (+13.1%) and communication services (+7.6%), with lower allocations primarily in healthcare (-5.6%) and industrials (--5.7%). This reflects the fact that most value ETFs hold small positions in higher-valuation sectors of technology and communication services, instead favoring defensive sectors like healthcare, with four ETFs in the group (VTV, IWD, IVE, and MOAT) allocating over 15% of their total assets to this sector.

Valuations Lower Than The Broader Market, But At The Upper Range Of The Peer Group

EPS's average price-to-earnings (P/E) ratio of 17.7x represents a discount of nearly 16% relative to the Russell 1000 Index, given below-average P/E ratios across sectors such as consumer discretionary and consumer staples. This is largely due to smaller positions in high-valuation players like Tesla (P/E 95.1x) and Costco (P/E 50.0x). On top of that, it also reflects EPS’s sector exposure, with underweight allocation to the high-multiple technology sector, while overweight in value areas like financials and energy.

Meanwhile, although it may be seen as a contradiction for a fund focused on profitable companies, EPS‘s profitability measures are, in aggregate, worse than the Russell 1000 index, with an EBITDA margin nearly five percentage points lower and ROE over ten percentage points lower than the broader equity index. This is primarily a byproduct of its lower allocation to high-margin tech stocks such as NVIDIA (EBITDA margin 63.5%), Meta Platform (49.9%), and Microsoft (52.8%), as well as in other areas such as Mastercard (61.6%) and Eli Lilly (39.8%). In addition, margins are also negatively influenced by lower margins seen in EPS’s holdings in the energy sector, like Exon (20.5%), Chevron (21.0%), and Marathon Petroleum (9.2%).

Compared to the peer group though, EPS has the second highest price-to-earnings ratio, just below MOAT (P/E 19.1x). This is driven by the peer group’s limited allocations to technology and consumer discretionary, particularly value-oriented ETFs like VTV, IWD, and IVE, which favor low-multiple sectors, such as financial services.

Returns Not Great, Moderate Volatility

EPS has not delivered compelling returns, as the fund has not kept pace with the Russell 1000 index, especially in longer time frames. Meanwhile, EPS outpaced Value ETFs like VTV, IWD, and IVE, performing generally in line with FNDX, MOAT, and PRF, with stronger returns in the short term. EPS has shown moderate volatility over time, with a beta of 0.94, slightly below the benchmark but relatively higher than value ETFs, due to their limited exposure to technology and consumer discretionary sectors.

With a relatively low daily volume of $2.2 million, shares of EPS trade at a still acceptable average spread of 0.06%, though it is slightly higher than the peer group. Meanwhile, its expense ratio of 0.08% is well below peers, despite a high turnover ratio of 102%. EPS’s dividend yield is one of the lowest in the peer group, especially compared to value ETFs. On the other hand, EPS has kept its tracking error below 3%, almost three times lower than peers, while its Sharpe ratio has been in the middle of the pack over the past three years, but still outperforming in the long term. These tradability metrics paint a generally positive picture for EPS, with mixed adjusted returns amid a low expense ratio and below-average tracking errors.

Final Thoughts

Broadly speaking, while EPS’s tradability and volatility measures are not bad, its overall performance has only slightly surpassed that of value ETFs, underperforming the Russell 1000 index. This is somewhat disappointing, as a fund with large positions in technology would be expected to deliver a more noticeable outperformance over time. This has likely been a function of EPS‘s underweight allocation to the “magnificent seven” stocks, which is also a key factor contributing to the fund’s still modest valuation and lower aggregate portfolio profitability.

That said, I view EPS as an investment vehicle that offers broad exposure to the equity market, but is not as heavily influenced by the surge in market capitalization seen in many companies during the bull market in place. This may be seen as a positive for this fund, as it is expected to limit the downside risk, particularly as valuations in some areas of the market seem elevated at this point after recent price gains.

In closing, while I view EPS as a valuable option for investors concerned about the increasing participation of mega caps in the broader indexes, those may look elsewhere for opportunities to outperform the broader market.

data.title: Customers Bancorp: Despite Some Troubles, The Firm Is Still Worth Considering
data.description:
When it comes to financial institutions, I typically prefer smaller enterprises. One company that tilts toward the higher end of the spectrum of those that I analyze in this space is Customers Bancorp (NYSE:CUBI). It's far from a giant in the financial sector. But with a market capitalization of $1.50 billion as of this writing, it's no slouch either. Historically, I have been bullish on the business. In fact, when I last wrote about the enterprise back in January of this year, I reaffirmed it as a ‘buy’ candidate. Even though the stock had skyrocketed by 49.1% since my previous article, significantly outperforming the S&P 500, I felt as though additional upside was on the table still.

Unfortunately, things have not gone exactly according to plan. Since then, shares have actually dropped by 9.8%. That is far worse than the 19.8% increase experienced by the S&P 500 over the same window of time. As disappointing as this is to see, I am at least happy that, since my initial ‘buy’ rating was instituted back in August 2023, shares are still outperforming the market. They are currently up 36.9% compared to the 32% experienced by the broader market. Even though the stock has not performed to my expectations in recent months, shares remain attractively priced, and asset quality remains high enough to warrant further optimism. Because of this, I have decided to keep the company rated a ‘buy’ for now.

The picture remains robust

The first thing that I would like to start with when it comes to this particular candidate is its balance sheet. This is an area where, unfortunately, we have seen a bit of weakness. Total deposits in the second quarter of the 2024 fiscal year, which is the most recent quarter for which we have data, came in at $17.68 billion. This represents a decline from the $17.92 billion the institution had at the end of 2023. This was driven entirely by a $1.09 billion decline in time deposits. For those not familiar, a time deposit account is a type of interest-bearing account that often pays a higher interest rate but, in exchange, requires the depositors to keep the funds in the account for a predetermined amount of time.

In some cases, time deposits can require a lockdown for just a few months. But in other cases, the requirement can be for several years. In fact, as of the end of the most recent quarter, Customers Bancorp had $120.2 million worth of time deposits that don't mature until after the year 2028. For the rest of this year, the business does have another $638.2 million worth of time deposits maturing. And in all likelihood, much of these will probably be recycled into savings accounts or other similar setups. In a way, this recycling proves beneficial for the company. This recycling into other types of accounts is likely occurring because of the high interest rate environment that we have had to deal with. And investors can probably expect more of it unless interest rates drop rather quickly.

Even though the value of deposits dropped, uninsured deposit exposure remains only a little above the 30% maximum threshold that I typically prefer. Right now, it's at 33.9%. I would like for it to be lower. But it's close enough that it's not a dealbreaker. Other parts of the balance sheet have seen increases in recent months. For instance, loans went from $12.73 billion at the end of 2023 to $13.12 billion in the most recent quarter. The value of securities did drop slightly from $3.51 billion to $3.47 billion, while the value of cash and cash equivalents declined from $3.85 billion to $3.05 billion. I don't like seeing these declines. But not everything here was bad. In fact, the firm did see a decline in debt on its books. In the most recent quarter, debt was $1.32 billion. This is down from the $1.51 billion that it had at the end of 2023. Furthermore, it's a drop from the $1.84 billion of the debt that the bank had in the third quarter of last year, which was the quarter that we had access to data from when I last wrote about the company.

To be honest with you, the balance sheet could be doing better. And it's because of that fact that I don't see Customers Bancorp as a stellar opportunity. However, it is achieving some impressive results elsewhere. Net interest income in the first half of this year was $292.8 million. That's comfortably above the $271.9 million reported one year earlier. The reduction in debt, combined with an expansion in its net interest margin from 3.06% to 3.20%, played a big role in this growth. The firm also benefited from a reduction in provision for credit losses from $43.2 million last year to $35.2 million this year, and from the absence of $6.3 million in interest expense associated with Federal Reserve advances. The company also saw expansion elsewhere.

Non-interest income managed to jump from $34.1 million in the first half of 2023 to $52.3 million the same time this year. This was mostly because, last year, the company reported a $5 million loss on the sale of capital call lines of credit. And this year, it benefited from an $11 million unrealized gain on certain equity method investments. The increase in non-interest income, combined with a rise in net interest income, helped the company grow its net profits from $94.3 million to $100.2 million. The picture would have been better had it not been for certain non-interest expense items, such as increased salaries and employee benefits costs, as well as certain other miscellaneous items.

If we assume that the rest of this year looks like the first half of this year did on a year-over-year basis, then net profits for 2024 will be somewhere around $250.1 million. But because of the irregular items that contributed to the profit increase this year, I would prefer to value the company based on results from 2023. This would give us net profits of $235.4 million. In the chart above, you can see that this translates to a price to earnings multiple for the Bank of 6.4. This is near the low end of the range that I love to see, which is for a price to earnings multiple of between 6 and 10. As the chart illustrates, Customers Bancorp is actually cheaper than five other similar banks that I compared it to. But of course, there are other ways to value a bank. In the table below, I compared it to the same five companies on both a price to book basis and price to tangible book basis. On a price to book basis, two of the five firms were cheaper than it. When using the price to tangible book scenario, Customers Bancorp is cheapest.

Another important thing to take into consideration is asset quality. In this regard, Customers Bancorp is somewhat mixed. As the first chart below illustrates, it has a decent return on assets figure of 1%. However, three of the five companies that I compared it to have readings that are higher than it is. But when we look at the picture through the lens of return on equity instead, we find that the 12.39% reading that Customers Bancorp boasts places it at the top of the class.

Takeaway

As things stand, Customers Bancorp looks pretty decent. The company is in no way a home run. But shares are cheap on a price to earnings basis and a price to book and price to tangible book bases. Asset quality is mixed, but not bad. I would be more optimistic if it weren't for the fact that certain balance sheet items are showing continued declines. That is something that investors should definitely pay careful attention to. But for now, the picture is not concerning enough for me to downgrade from the ‘hold’ I currently have the company rated.

data.title: An Unconventional Case For Being Bullish On Snowflake
data.description:
Am I crazy?

I’m going to say “Buy” for shares of data-company Snowflake (NYSE: SNOW).

The stock’s P/E FWD is near 200. Even Nvidia (NVDA), the Wall Street face of AI hype, isn’t that expensive. The latter’s P/E is a mere 49.

You can judge my sanity.

But before I defend myself, let me start by at least saying I’m not ignorant. I admit that by any numerical valuation metric, the stock looks badly overpriced.

The company is one of today’s big-name tech-growth companies. And as with many others, AI is relevant. SNOW is into data… big, big, big, big data.

Readers familiar with my work know I’ll accept a very high ratio if the Proj. 3-5Y EPS Gr figure is also high. For SNOW, the consensus growth rate projection is 23.31%. That’s terrific. But it’s not terrific enough. The PEG FWD is 8.8.

I’m skipping my usual tabular value presentation. (I typically compare SNOW to medians for the SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY) and the industry, in this case it would be Internet Services and Infrastructure.

You don’t need all that to roll your eyes at a P/E near 200. You know it’s stratospheric.

Now, suppose this emerging growth company was just barely breaking into the black. Maybe it’s EPS amounts to pennies per share.

Suppose the forward estimate of EPS is only $0.03 per share. Perhaps it was negative $.85 a year earlier. And suppose the Street expects $1.20 in the next year. If it were anything like that, a 200 PE FWD might not faze me.

But that’s not SNOW. Here are its quarterly trends.

Perhaps a strong combination of growth and EBITDA Margin could support a high EV/Sales ratio. This is a good metric for emerging companies that don’t yet have normal profits.

SNOW’s EV/Sales (FWRD) ratio is 11.75. That, too, is very high. NVDA’s ratio is 26.31. So perhaps there’s hope for SNOW.

Actually, this idea fails.

SNOW’s EBITDA margin is -35.25%. (Are you wondering about a negative margin and positive EPS? That table shows normalized. EPS. But GAAP EPS in the red. I’ll discuss below.) NVDA’s EBITDA margin is 63.53%!

And by the way, a negative EBITDA (sometimes called Operating) Margin can be a red flag in and of itself.

I understand that very young companies can need time to outgrow relatively fixed overhead. When they do, the margin will turn positive.

But until they do, I typically adopt a prove-it wait-and-see stance. I tend to analogize such companies to adult kids who haven’t yet moved away from their parents’ basements.

It takes a lot to get me interested in a basement dweller. Here, however…

William Miller’s AMZN Experience Inspires the Framework Through Which I’ll Rate SNOW as a “Buy”

Miller is a well-known value-oriented investor. Yet he owned Amazon.com (AMZN) in 1999. (Actually, he bought through the 1997 IPO, sold it, regretted having done so, and then repurchased in 1999.)

He may well be the most famous long-term AMZN bull. He experienced many angst-producing ups and downs. Ultimately, Miller succeeded. (See my 7/13/24 AMZN post for a sense of how successful long-term AMZN holdings fared.)

Miller’s bold moves attract interesting interviews. He’s very bullish on Bitcoin now. I won’t comment since I’m clueless about all things crypto.

Regarding company stocks, Miller typically recites the standard quant lingo. He discusses companies’ potential to earn long-term returns that outstrip the cost of capital. And I expect he likely has spreadsheet models like that.

But I think such models are inherently non-credible for truly emerging businesses. I explain this view in my 5/28/24 NVDA writeup.

I won’t try to read Miller’s mind. So, I don’t know if he privately agrees. But some quotes suggest such modeling may not be the be all and end all for his AMZN-like choices.

Whether that’s so or not, we can still look to Miller’s approach to AMZN. That will set a context for the non-traditional way I’ll assess SNOW.

In 2015, Motley Fool wrote as follows regarding Miller’s AMZN investment:

Miller isn't afraid to throw valuation metrics aside. He pointed out that Amazon may not have looked attractive based on traditional measures (price-to-earnings, price-to-book, etc.), but "that speaks more to the weakness of those methods than to the fundamental risk- reward relationships of those businesses." Essentially, traditional measures only work with traditional companies. So, when a disruptive Internet company like Amazon arrives, you end up with its growth potential being compared to brick-and-mortar book stores. This was a clear misunderstanding of what Amazon could be, and that led many investors to view Amazon as overvalued.

In 2019, Institutional Investor wrote as follows about an interview with Miller:

Many value investors use accounting-based techniques to determine valuations.

“But that’s very dangerous, because [generally accepted accounting principles] are designed to capture the underlying economics of a business that’s consistent across all industries,” Miller argues. “That language changes slowly.”

It also means that GAAP-based research often doesn’t measure businesses well, he says.

“People missed Amazon because they said it doesn’t make money,” he explains. “GAAP isn’t divinely inspired.” Miller doesn’t ignore GAAP- based numbers — but he views them simply as inputs to figure out the true value of a business.

The longtime money manager thinks he also benefits from an analytical advantage. “I didn’t have my own information on Amazon. My view was just that people were looking at it incorrectly.”

Miller partly credits behavioral analysis for his success. “Social psychologists have shown how groups of people behave, weighing dramatic information more than non-dramatic, being too short-term- oriented, etc.,” he says. “We try to exploit all those things.”

On August 26, 2004, Forbes wrote thusly about its interview of Miller:

I’d gotten to know Jeff Bezos a little bit and I was very impressed with the way that they thought about the business and what they were doing…. It was a retailer, but Amazon didn’t have stores. They had warehouses and shipped direct to the customer. That was Dell’s model. Amazon’s metrics didn’t look anything like Walmart or Home Depot. They were exactly like Dell—same operating margins, same gross margins, same low-cost overhead, same high inventory turnovers.

I hope a picture of how I’ll be approaching SNOW is starting to come into focus. Numbers will help. But they won’t dominate.

How SNOW Got Going

Today, we take the cloud for granted. But in 2015, Peter Wagner, founding partner Wing (a venture capital firm that backed SNOW early on), asked a panel of IT execs when data analytics would move to the cloud. Their answer: “Uh, never.”

That was consistent with how Oracle (ORCL) co-founder Larry Ellison saw the world. My 10/11/14 article on Broadcom (AVGO) quoted his now famous anti-cloud rant. “It's complete gibberish. It's insane. When is this idiocy going to stop?”

Who could doubt Ellison? After all, on-premises enterprise-scale relational databases ruled.

The relational element is what makes big data click. Simply (very simply) put, imagine a customer data set and also, a purchase order data set.

The company needs to track both. It would be unwieldy to put everything in one giant data table. So, they’re kept separate.

But one piece of information is common to both tables… a Customer ID. This “key” lets the tables “relate” to one another.

That makes it easy for the company to track customer behavior, customer accounts, inventory, and product popularity as a whole and based on region and customer demographics.

There’s a huge legion of data analysts, database administrators and SQL programers that keep this stuff running.

But not long after the IT panel scoffed to Wagner about the cloud, enterprises stepped up efforts to move data there. We now understand why…

Data work was freed from specific locations. And companies found it better to pay cloud services to worry about servers, maintenance, updates, etc. It really helped that the cloud learned to handle security and privacy.

But Oracle wasn’t leading the way. Industry observers wondered if (premises bound) relational databases would die out.

Eventually open-source outfits like Apache and Hadoop became the rage. Analytics became the new big thing.

Meanwhile, Benoit Dageville, Thierry Cruanes, two veteran Oracle architects who saw the cloud-future decided to leave. They, together with Marcin Żukowski, a data warehousing expert who had startup experience, formed Snowflake in 2012.

Early on, many wondered if the newcomer would be crushed by AMZN. Snowflake built its offering on top of AWS. But Amazon introduced its own cloud warehouse, Redshift.

Skipping all the gory details, Snowflake survived, and then some…

SNOW’s Modern Approach to Data

Unlike Redshift and others, SNOW’s offering was built from scratch for (native to) the cloud. It wasn’t and isn’t shackled by an old on-premises heritage.

SNOW was able to, and did, make it easier for users to implement and manage their clouds.

And they can do so where they want. They’re not captive to AWS or any other hyperscaler. (SNOW is the database. Someone else, like AWS, runs the cloud that houses and serves it.)

SNOW separates data compute from data storage. This may not seem crucial to ordinary folks. But it’s huge for data pros.

Each can be managed and maintained with full flexibility. Actions done within one don’t burden, or slow, the other. Faults in one are corrected without impacting the other.

And users pay only for what they actually use. They don’t pay for idle resources.

This means SNOW’s revenues are based on usage. It doesn’t have the recurring subscription revenue streams many crave. But for SNOW, it makes sense given likely growth.

Today, more than 90% of businesses use the cloud. But not all are all in. Only 60% of data is stored there.

Day-to-day computation has a long growth runway.

“Big data” (data sets too large for conventional software) is opening up more analytic possibilities for more users.

So, too, is unstructured data that doesn’t fit normal row-column format. Examples include graphics, text documents, handwritten notes, and videos.

And, of course, there’s AI. That’s still in the early stage of being developed.

It would seem impossible to set subscription rates that wouldn’t wind up undercharging or overcharging different users at different times.

And more data is collected every day. So even without new uses, volume alone is mushrooming.

Usage-based revenues let SNOW grow along with the new digital world. That’s the sort of potential that makes a long-term growth case for the company.

And by building from scratch, SNOW is able to let its clients escape the confines of silos.

These are data repositories built and secured by and for a particular user group.

An example would be a separate database for sales, human resources, and product. Someone evaluating salesperson performance may need to determine commission or bonuses. That would touch all three data repositories. But siloed data is often incompatible with data in other silos.

I don’t have the background to supply here a full course in data science. But I hope you can recognize the merits of cloud flexibility and scalability from built from scratch.

That’s far better than adapting from old, legacy, on-premises offerings. Don’t underestimate how much time and effort many data users, even today, spend squeezing round pegs into square holes. SNOW users need not do that.

SNOW is constantly adding new things.

It was originally into the data warehouse model. Those “store large amounts of current and historical data from various sources. They contain a range of data, from raw ingested data to highly curated, cleansed, filtered, and aggregated data.”

SNOW’s Iceberg offering helps users effectively manage data lakes. These “store large amounts of structured, semi-structured, and unstructured data….Data does not need to be transformed in order to be added… efficiently without upfront planning…. (This flexibility) enables business analysts and data scientists to look for unexpected patterns and insights.”

The company’s Cortex AI offering helps users build generative AI models.

SNOW is building specialized versions of its offering for specific industry verticals. It already has platforms for financial services, healthcare, retailers, tech, media, education, telecomm, and the public sector.

And the company’s Snowflake Marketplace offers a variety of third-party apps. These aren’t two-kids-in-a-garage type things. FactSet (FDS), a world class data vendor, is available through SNOW’s marketplace.

Competition, Especially from Databricks

SNOW isn’t alone in its business. Others offer cloud-based data warehousing and data lakes.

As noted above in connection with AMZN’s Redshift, there’s something to be said for SNOW’s independence. For the most part rivals need to be used in a particular platform.

The industry has evolved to the point where competitive analysis needs to focus mainly on Snowflake versus privately-owned Databricks. (I’m planning a separate article that will address Oracle (ORCL), the giant that sort-of spawned SNOW.)

Bare bones feature lists seem identical. SNOW and Databricks now compete head-to-head pretty much across the board.

But they evolved differently. And that gives each a distinct feel.

As discussed, SNOW has a relational database heritage. It added, and continues to add, heavy-duty analytics. (Cortex is an example.)

Databricks came from the opposite direction.

It was born from AMPLab. That was a University California at Berkeley big data analytics project. One such development was open-source analytics-engine Apache Spark.

The Apache Spark founders launched Databricks. The former became the heart of the latter.

I assume you see where this is going.

Snowflake started with data and built its way into analytics. Databricks started with analytics and built its way into data. And now the two face off in the competitive arena.

So, which is better? Which will win?

I doubt there’s a clear answer. And there may not be one for a long time, if ever.

One possibility is having two big gorillas dominate the business the way AWS and Microsoft (MSFT) Azure do in cloud. The market seems big enough for both. As recently as 2022, Gartner estimated that only 30% of workloads (distinct from data) have gone cloud.

Future M&A is possible. (There are no rumors. It’s just me imagining.)

If I’m still writing when (or if) Databricks goes public, I’ll probably write about it too. And I may recommend owning both shares… like a data-leaders portfolio.

My research thus far finds little in the way of commentators or experts willing to pick one winner.

Many seem to favor both (or all) of the above. Some suggest that SNOW is for database geeks, while Databricks is for analytics nerds. But few seem willing to really pound the table for one as opposed to the other.

One exception I found is Harry Glaser. He’s co-founder and CEO a firm that “makes infrastructure for experienced ML teams to deploy large, complex models into their products very quickly.”

His 8/17/24 blog notes SNOW's cloud-native heritage and its more modern database. Glaser ends by writing, “Databricks has strengths. But if offered a choice, I’d take a share of Snowflake every time.”

SNOW’s Numbers

See above for tables depicting the quarterly Sales and EPS trends

The NM (No Meaningful figure) designations in the historical growth table below should come as no surprise. Negative numbers will do that when computing growth rates.

The forward growth table should likewise come as no surprise considering the above discussion.

Returns on equity and assets, which I usually show, are not meaningful here.

The most important fundamental to mention is debt. It’s very modest. For SNOW, it amounts to just 2.31 times free cash flow. Industry and SPY medians are 6.35 and 7.85 respectively.

So, all things, considered I think the world has bigger fish to fry than fretting over SNOW’s $2 billion convertible notes placement last month.

We definitely need to consider the basement dweller negative EBITDA (Operating) margins.

Absent employee stock compensation, these would be positive today.

I’ll bet that makes you think about future dilution when the company issues a lot more shares to those folks. Yes, it’s not ideal. But it’s commonplace in emerging tech.

Many employees at such firms are willing to trade some cash now for stakes in the future they help build.

If you object, SNOW, and probably a lot of emerging tech, aren’t for you.

As to the core economics of the business, look at how SNOW’s trailing 12-month gross margin compares with those of other big-name companies.

SNOW is not number one. But it’s standing is very respectable. That’s especially given that SNOW is so new. The others are all very well established.

Look now at highlights from SNOW’s income statements. I refer to this as the “business” portion of the statement. (It excludes non-business items such as capital costs, M&A-related costs, tax strategies, etc.)

Look at the gross margin trend. Much Cost of Sales is variable. So, it’s hard for increases in sales to automatically boost gross margin. Changes tend to be gradual as companies get more or less efficient. I’m satisfied with SNOW’s gradual improvement.

Operating (or EBITDA) margin is very different. These costs are largely fixed. Much is determined by company choice.

Notice first, how the operating margin is trending upward. That’s what I need to see when it’s below zero.

Second, notice the impact of R&D.

This is purely discretionary. And at this stage of its life, we WANT the company to be doing a lot of this. It’s still building up its capabilities.

We also want the company to be spending for customer acquisition.

The only catch is whether the company can afford this.

We see here, in highlights from SNOW’s annual Cash Flow statements that it can well afford to keep building its business.

Cash from Operations would already be in the black without non-cash charges (such as employee stock compensation).

Cash from Investing reflects share buybacks. Those seem wise now. SNOW will have to give employees lots of stock. And Wall Street already pushed the price down.

Notice too how the annual change in cash (which, including cash equivalents and short-term investments, was $3.3 billion at the end of the latest quarter). That’s great for an early-stage company.

Note, too, the trend in free cash flow.

Also, management mentioned on the last earnings call that “[w]e continue to see approximately 80% of our customers paying annually in advance.” That helps! Customers wouldn't do that if they worried about SNOW’s viability.

SNOW has nothing in common with the nonsense-companies that imploded during the circa-2000 dot-com bubble. SNOW is not cash-burning its way to oblivion.

Meanwhile, the last earnings release landed on the Street with an unpleasant thud. The stock dropped 14.7% on August 22, the next day.

Stuff happens. Companies are watching spending now.

And management cited an unnamed security breach (not involving SNOW) that gave customers pause. Psst... I’ll bet they were referring to CrowdStrike (CRWD).

My eyes really rolled at some of the analysts referred to in the Seeking Alpha news story.

[T]he fiscal second quarter results were good, but perhaps not enough.

****

[W]aning optimization headwinds and emerging new product initiatives should support growth, but investors may need more concrete signals to get onboard.

I wonder if any of those analysts were ever involved in building a new business. It’s bumpy.

Concrete signals are for established businesses with established operations. Sensible forecasting requires historical experience (precedent, data).

(Back in my Reuters days, I tried to push a premium stock screener aimed at individual investors. My boss asked how much revenue it would generate in five years. I said, “I’ll let you know in five years.” The rest of the conversation did not go well.)

Newness necessarily involves doing, experiencing, learning and adjustment. Many call it trial and error.

Absent that, the emerging part of emerging growth couldn’t exist. Those who invest in this theme need to accept that.

Risks

One big risk involves competition.

The other involves the quarterly guidance/reporting angst. Investors who demand “concrete signals” will periodically find reasons to dump stock, as they did on August 22nd.

I covered those in the preceding sections.

What to do About SNOW Stock

The price chart tells us many on the Street are uncomfortable with SNOW

The stock’s big downslope speaks for itself.

In recent days, the 10-day exponential moving average (EMA) turned up and toward the 50-day EMA. But the very latest price action suggests that may not last.

Consider, too, Chaikin Money Flow (CMF) and the Chaikin Oscillator (CO). Both measure which party to trades is more motivated. CMF does it for institutional investors. CO does it for the market in general.

Both indicators tell us sellers are more motivated. So short term, the stock still faces headwinds.

As noted above, investors hated the latest earnings report.

Clearly, SNOW is only for risk-tolerant contrarians with long time horizons. You need the sort of mindset early AMZN investors, like William Miller, had.

As I’ve said before, my investment stance depends mainly on whether I think a stock will be better than, in line with, or worse than market.

Here’s how I apply that to the Seeking Alpha rating system:

  • “Strong Buy” means I see the stock as being better than the market and I’m bullish about the direction of the market.
  • “Buy” means I see the stock as being better than the market but am not confident about the market’s near-term direction.
  • “Hold” means I see the stock as moving in line with the market.
  • “Sell” means I see the stock as being worse than the market but am not confident about the market’s near-term direction.
  • “Strong Sell” means I see the stock as being worse than the market and I’m bearish about the direction of the market.

For those whose mindsets are akin to those that motivated Miller with his investment in AMZN, I apply this scale to rate SNOW as a “Buy.”

That said, SNOW is out there on the reward-risk scale. So, carefully consider your risk tolerance and investment horizon.

Speaking for myself, I’m too old to take on that much risk. So, I don’t and won’t personally own SNOW. But I want to make a case for those whose own investment situations may be different.

data.title: Wall Street Brunch: Tesla Earnings Steal The Spotlight
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify.

Will the numbers improve sentiment after disappointing robotaxi event? (0:17) Durable goods orders expected to drop. (2:16) Boeing has a tentative agreement with union to end strike. (2:56)

The following is an abridged transcript:

Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA) earnings highlight this week as reporting season gains steam.

Analysts expect the EV maker to post revenue of $29.73 billion and EPS of $0.86 when it issues results on Wednesday. Gross margin is expected to come in at 18.77%.

For the conference call, a point of major emphasis will be Elon Musk’s commentary on the timing of a new mass-market cheaper electric vehicle, as well as the latest updates on FSD, energy storage business and robotaxi developments.

Seeking Alpha analyst Richard Durant, who has a Sell rating on the stock, says the business “faces a critical 12-18 month period, as its investments in self-driving technology are reaching a point where significant progress will be needed for the company to maintain its valuation premium.”

“This is a risky setup, as it is largely unknown, how self-driving performance will improve with access to more data, but the results aren't particularly encouraging so far.”

But in the bullish camp, Oliver Rodzianko says the recent “We, Robot” event was a success, despite the precipitant stock decline.

“Not only does it show Tesla investors and customers that the company's plans have a substantial grounding, but the company should be commended for how quickly it embraced a full pivot to autonomous taxis,” he said. “Although Musk previously announced that Tesla's robotaxis would be on the road by 2020, this doesn't diminish the groundbreaking achievement of being able to showcase a fully autonomous driving experience in 2024 in a state-of-the-art vehicle design.”

Also on the earnings calendar, on Monday, Nucor (NUE) and Logitech (LOGI) report.

General Electric (GE), Philip Morris (PM), Verizon (VZ), Texas Instruments (TXN), Lockheed Martin (LMT), General Motors (GM) and 3M (MMM) weigh in on Tuesday.

Joining Tesla on Wednesday are Coca-Cola (KO), T-Mobile (TMUS), IBM (IBM), AT&T (T) and Boeing (BA).

Valero (VLO), UPS (UPS), Honeywell (HON), American Airlines (AAL), Northrop Grumman (NOC), Southwest Airlines (LUV), and Dow (DOW) issue results on Thursday.

On Friday, Centene (CNC), HCA (HCA) and Colgate-Palmolive (CL) are up.

On the economic front, September durable goods orders will garner the most attention. The report hits Friday, with economists expecting a -0.9% decline in the headline number and a -0.1% drop in core orders, ex transportation.

Wells Fargo economists say: “Durable goods orders have been volatile in recent months primarily due to swings in aircraft orders.”

“The strong start to the Fed's easing cycle provides scope for a rebound in equipment spending in the year ahead,” they add. “That said, it will likely take some time for lower rates to filter through the economy and revitalize business demand for durable goods. Looking to September, a decline in industrial production foretells an underwhelming durable goods print.”

In the news this weekend, a union representing around 30,000 Boeing (BA) employees announced a tentative agreement that, if finalized, could end a more-than-month-long strike that has disrupted production.

As part of the deal, Boeing is set to offer its workers a 35% pay hike over four years, sharply higher than its initial offer of 25%, the International Association of Machinists said. Other terms of the deal include a reinstated incentive plan and improved contributions to employees' 401(k) plans.

“We look forward to our employees voting on the negotiated proposal,” Boeing said.

In addition, Boeing is deciding whether it will sell assets such as underperforming business units to raise cash as it seeks to overcome its recent crises. That’s according to The Wall Street Journal.

The company’s board met last week at the company’s headquarters in Arlington, Va., where they questioned division heads and combed through reports on the condition of each unit.

CEO Kelly Ortberg, who was brought in to fix the company’s operations in early August, wasn’t there for the discussions as he was in strike negotiations.

And happy hour is over.

TGI Friday’s, the bar/restaurant that is given credit for popularizing that term for discount drinks, is looking for financing as it prepares to file for bankruptcy in the coming weeks.

Bloomberg says the company is in talks with lenders to search for a loan that would let it continue running its restaurants through the Chapter 11 process and emerge as an operating company. TGI Friday’s joins the long list of casual dining chains struggling with consumers' shifting preference for fast-casual eateries like Chipotle (CMG).

The TGI Friday’s on Manhattan’s Upper East Side was one of the world’s first singles bar and it was also where Brian Flanagan (played by Tom Cruise) learned to bartend in “Cocktail.”

For income investors, companies that have an ex-dividend date coming next week include Caterpillar (CAT), CVS Health (CVS), Lowe’s (LOW), Clorox (CLX), and Fastenal (FAST).

Companies forecast to increase their quarterly dividend payouts include Smurfit Westrock (SW) to $0.33 from $0.3025, Hartford Financial Services Group (HIG) to $0.50 from $0.47, Entergy (ETR) to $1.20 from $1.13 and American Electric Power (AEP) to $0.92 from $0.88.

And in the Wall Street Research Corner, UBS Global Wealth Management said it now has an attractive view on U.S. equities, supported in part by growing adoption of artificial intelligence technology.

David Lefkowitz, head of equities Americas, says: “From a macro perspective, the combination of slowing but durable economic growth, healthy earnings growth, and continued Fed rate cuts are all supportive.”

While economic growth is slowing, the country’s labor market is “healthy,” with real wages rising, he said.

He lists his top picks, but it’s good to point out that UBS has had a neutral view on U.S. equities while the S&P 500 has risen more than 20% year to date.

Among the names are Meta (META), Home Depot (HD), Ralph Lauren (RL), Coca-Cola (KO), ExxonMobil (XOM), BofA (BAC), Merck (MRK), Microsoft (MSFT), Freeport-McMoRan (FCX), Equity Residential (EQR) and NextEra Energy (NEE).

data.title: A&W: Valuation Gets Ambitious As Risks Go Vertical
data.description:
Note: All values are in CAD unless noted otherwise. All stock prices refer to the TSX unless noted otherwise.

It is tough finding picks from a value investor's standpoint in this market. You have some quality stocks trading at some of the highest nosebleed levels. You also have lower quality stocks that appear cheap and then get slammed as management repeatedly fails to deliver. So patience is key in the hunt. After a lot of patience, we finally pivoted to buy ratings on A&W (TSX:AW:CA) and bought some shares.

The rationale was that the sound valuation and a complete insulation from key risks facing burger chains, made this a tasty snack. The stock delivered as management completed a rather unusual transaction, converting the royalty company into a quick-service-restaurant model.

Where Things Stand

One of the key things to understand here is that we felt A&W was attractive in its royalty format, because of two things. The first being that it sported a near 6.8% yield when we hit the buy ratings. Considering that A&W distributed all its earnings, we were looking at a 15X earnings multiple. That multiple was attractive as A&W had no labor costs risks and no risks of a margin compression. It also carried very little debt. Even the buyout paid some tribute to this thinking by assigning 41% of the new company ownership to the 3% A&W royalty rights.

Keeping things in perspective here, let's see what has happened. The stock has rocketed over $40.00 per share, even higher than the cash offer. So even on the old "lower-risk" model, we would get just a 4.8% yield versus the 6.8% where we gave this a green light. Considering the current economic conditions in Canada, we would give anything under a 6% yield as a "hold" and a 5% yield as a "sell". Again, all of that is on the old royalty model. But here we have pole vaulted into a bunch of new risks. Wage inflation is a clear and present danger, especially with the Government clamping down on some bad behavior by corporations to dodge wage hikes. Margin compression is almost certainly on its way in the next recession. The company has taken on a large load of debt at the same time.

▪ ~3.0x net debt (incl. net leases) / 2023 combined adjusted EBITDA(3), with up to $325 million revolving credit facility from CIBC and RBC ▪ Proceeds to be used to fund the cash consideration of the transaction and repay current indebtedness of the Fund and A&W Food Services.

Source: A&W

Q3-2024

The most recent results were the ones just prior to this transaction. They reflect the older low-risk model in its entirety. They were not pretty. Royalty income was up just 0.3% year over year in Q3-2024.

Royalty Pool Same Store Sales Growth is a function of changes in guest counts and check size, which are impacted by party size, menu prices and menu mix and changes in consumers discretionary spending. The Royalty Pool Same Store Sales Growth(i) of -1.0% for the third quarter and nil year to date primarily results from a decrease in guest counts partially offset by an increase in average check size due in part to industry-wide inflation on goods, services, and labour. The decrease in guest counts reflects A&W's proportionate share of an overall decline in traffic at burger quick service restaurants ("QSR") across Canada. Food Services believes the decline in traffic at A&W restaurants and burger QSR more generally is attributable to increased interest rates and inflation, along with market uncertainty, which have impacted consumer discretionary spending. In response to these economic conditions, Food Services continues to seek new and innovative ways to offer A&W's guests a delicious and affordable experience and in turn increase guest traffic.

Source: A&W

Some other key numbers from that press release are highlighted below.

To be fair, the above results do include $2.46 million of one time transaction costs related to this new setup. In the absence of that, the distribution would be covered. Nonetheless, the same store sales are pointing to some early signs of a pushback from the beaten consumer. We don't like using anecdotal evidence in general, but this is a place we dine at from time to time. The average price per person for a meal has doubled since 2020. Our experience is rather drastically different than what the management thinks about its current pricing.

Thomas Burke

Yes, hi, guys. Just -- well, a couple of things about the numbers. So are we seeing just obviously aside from the general market weakness, is there any idea about market share numbers? Is this really an industry thing? And how can we characterize this, because these numbers over the past few quarters are very uncharacteristic of the traditional A&W numbers since I've been following the company over 20 years?

Susan Senecal

Sure. I can comment on that. Thanks for the question. Yeah, obviously, I think the economic situation is part of the driver of this. We also noticed a really big desire for value and affordability and so on and so forth, which we need to address, it feels like, in a more deliberate way. So that's what we're focused on. And I think we're already starting to understand a little bit more about that need and how we might compete more effectively when it comes to giving even more value to our guests and making sure that for families and so on that we’re still very affordable. It's been a bit of a focus for the whole year, but we certainly are reemphasizing that in our -- in terms of our plans, especially going into this Q4.

Source: A&W Q3-2024 Conference Call Transcript, (emphasis ours)

We have certainly not seen this focus, and frankly we have seen far better value conscious pricing from Burger King (part of Restaurant Brands International Inc. (QSR)) and McDonald's Corporation (MCD), in Canada.

Verdict

We no longer own this.

Credit conditions are over easy today and debt is cheap. So it is possible the next 2-3 quarters don't exactly look bad under the new model. But unless there is some drastic price alignment, we think the core A&W customer is likely to run, or at least come in to dine less frequently. Optimistically, we would assign this a peak multiple of 16X potential sustainable earnings of about $2.00 per share. That gives us a fair value of $32.00 and even that assumes that they get some traction from the Pret Deal. An alternative valuation would be at 11X EV to EBITDA, using a base case of $90 million in EBITDA. These numbers give us a lower fair value of around $29.00 per share. We are starting the new shares at a Sell and will update our views once we see the next quarter's results.

Please note that this is not financial advice. It may seem like it, sound like it, but surprisingly, it is not. Investors are expected to do their own due diligence and consult with a professional who knows their objectives and constraints.

data.title: DBP: Gold And Silver Exposure In One Wrapper
data.description:
Gold has been on absolute fire for the past year, unrelentingly making new highs as it continues to, I believe, send a warning about global debt and increasingly fragile monetary systems. More recently, poor man’s Gold, i.e. Silver, has also been suddenly running hot with some major technical resistance levels finally being broken. I remain bullish on both. And if you are, as well, then you may want to consider the Invesco DB Precious Metals Fund ETF (NYSEARCA:DBP). This is an ETF that aims to follow the DBIQ Optimum Yield Precious Metals Index Total Return™. This index tracks how futures contracts perform for Gold and Silver. The fund's main goal is to give investors a cheap and easy way to invest in the precious metals market.

What makes DBP stand out is how it uses the Optimum Yield™ method. This approach tries to lessen the negative effects of contango and boost the good effects of backwardation. In other words, it's meant to dynamically manage the roll aspect of the future contracts to enhance return depending upon the shape of the futures term structure, which can have long-term return implications most investors may not otherwise consider as either an opportunity or risk.

A Look At The Holdings

Roughly 80% of the fund is made up of Gold futures. The fund puts so much weight on gold because it matters a lot in the precious metals market and can help keep things steady when the economy gets rocky. The remaining futures allocation goes to Silver.

Keep in mind that these percentages show the fund's futures contracts. The fund puts its collateral into high-quality government securities. The largest collateral allocation goes to the Invesco Government & Agency Portfolio. This is a money market fund that puts money into U.S. government securities and repurchase agreements. Next comes US Treasury Bills, followed by a short-duration.

Peer Comparison

Some key competitors include the SPDR Gold Shares (GLD), iShares Silver Trust (SLV), and the abrdn Physical Precious Metals Basket Shares ETF (GLTR). What makes DBP stand out is how it deals with futures contracts instead of actual metals directly. To the extent that the fund’s approach can generate excess returns by managing the term structure of Gold and Silver, respectively, during periods of contango and backwardation, there is theoretically potential for these funds to do better than spot returns.

In practice? When we look at the performance of DBP to GLD, SLV, and GLTR, we find that DBP has outperformed GLTR, but not outright owning GLD or SLV, which invest directly in the metals as opposed to the futures side.

Pros and Cons

On the plus side, it’s convenient. In one ETF, you have Gold and Silver in one trade (through futures). Because the fund uses futures contracts, you don't have to stress about keeping, insuring, or moving actual metal, which can be a hassle when you own it yourself if you want to avoid funds like GLD or SLV. What's more, DBP's Optimum Yield™ method aims to boost returns by fine-tuning the rolling of futures contracts. This approach can be useful in markets experiencing contango or backwardation giving DBP an advantage over ETFs that just track spot prices or use simpler rolling strategies.

The collateral investments in safe, interest-bearing securities also provide an extra source of return, which can be valuable when yields are low. This setup allows investors to profit from both changes in commodity prices and interest income.

But we need to think about the downsides too. Using futures contracts makes things tricky and might cause tracking errors. The fund might not match gold and silver spot prices, which can puzzle investors used to ETFs that actually invest in Gold and Silver physically. Also, the futures approach can lead to different tax dynamics than ETFs with physical metals.

Conclusion

It’s an interesting fund and approach, but candidly I’d prefer just blending GLD and SLV directly. Get direct access to the metal as opposed to futures, and rebalance accordingly. Despite that being my preference, there’s nothing fundamentally wrong with the fund. I think if you want convenience, this is a good way to get easy exposure to Gold and Silver. Just know that futures will always be more complicated than outright owning the underlying assets.

data.title: Life360: I Love It As A User, But Has A Lot To Prove And Valuation Is Expensive
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Life360 (NASDAQ:LIF) is a recently IPO'd company that offers location services and is trying to develop a range of products that will keep users loyal to the brand. It has grown a lot and has some interesting ideas in development, but aren't yet fully proven and the valuation is high, so there's no margin for error and it would have to be executed perfectly to offer appealing returns.

Although I'm a regular user of the product, I understand its value proposition and where it wants to go, I still don't feel confident enough to assign a buy rating.

The Core of Life360

At the core of Life360 there's a platform for mobile phones and offers location services. The idea is that families and friends create groups and share their location in real time and, in my experience as a user of the app, I can tell you about some of the features of the app that I value the most:

  • Location alerts: You can select predetermined locations and name them as you like (work/home/etc) so that when one of the members of the group arrives at the place, Life360 will send a notification to the members of the group.
  • Driving reports: When a member is driving, the app alerts of this, shows the speed and an estimated time at which they will arrive at the predetermined destinations. It also shows the history of recent travels.
  • Assistance in case of emergencies: Luckily I've never needed this option, but if there's an emergency the application has an SOS button that alerts your contacts, as well as a vehicle accident detector.

This platform basically offers the peace of mind of knowing where your loved ones are in case they have a car accident or are in a risk situation. For example, when my sister goes out to a party, it's starting to get late and she doesn't answer messages, I can simply log into Life360 and find out if she's still at the party, if she's already on her way, or raise alarms if she's in some strange location.

The disadvantage is that this app only works with the internet, so if your cell phone runs out of battery you would no longer have access to the real-time location, although at least you could know its last location in case something unfortunate has happened. Simply put, it's easy for users to become regulars on a platform that offers this level of security for their loved ones.

Monetization

While I like this app and I was part of the "word of mouth" with my family and friends, at first look it wouldn't be clear how Life360 could monetize its users, since I don't think it's worth paying for just a location service (my opinion).

Given this, one of the strategies that the company has adopted has been to include hardware in its product catalog through acquisitions. The two acquisitions are the following:

  • Tile, a location device which was acquired in 2022. This can be used on cell phones, keys and other objects that you want to have located and works through Bluetooth.
  • Jiobit, acquired in 2022, uses secure cellular, GPS, Wi-Fi, and Bluetooth connections to pinpoint children, pets or the elderly. This is more secure since it can track anything with any available signal and doesn't depend only on Wi-Fi like Life360 or Bluetooth like Tile.

In 2023 the hardware represented 19% of total sales growing 21.5% compared to the previous year, but you still have to download the Tile app separately to use it, the ideal would be that in the near future both applications would be unified to have everything in one place and thus make it worth paying for a subscription. It's worth mentioning that in this space the company has a giant rival in Apple, which also has a tracker and a location app. Coming integrated with iPhone makes it much more convenient and reliable for users.

Regarding the platform, it currently has a freemium model in which you can access most of the functions but are somewhat limited compared to the Silver and Gold version. For example, with the free version you can see two days of location history, while with the silver version you have up to seven days and in the gold version up to a whole month.

I think that the company will have a hard job making this subscription as attractive as possible, since the functionalities to add to a platform are rather few with current capacity. However, I see it as very positive that the company will begin to place ads in the free version and you can avoid them by paying the subscription, which I do see as an interesting path because the platform is already made and the only thing they have to do is find a space to position ads, get advertisers and there they'll have revenue with wide margins.

As far as the future is concerned, I find very interesting what can be achieved in the "pets" segment and if the Tile functionalities are incorporated within the same application. As for what the company says, they see growth potential in auto insurance, family financial services and elder care which are usually recurring services and with high switching costs if the concept is proven correct.

As a fun fact, in the memberships Life360 is offering a Tile product as a gift. Also, I received this notification saying that if I pay for my gold membership they will give me six months of Headspace, a platform that provides guided meditation sessions. It's not of my special interest, but you can notice the efforts to make the membership attractive through functionality, hardware gifts and even partnerships.

High Growth, High Margin

Between 2018 and 2023, the company's revenue has grown at a very high 59% annually on average. This can be broken down into a growth in monthly active users of 28% annually and revenue per active user of 31%. For example, at the end of 2018 the company had 19M active users per month who spent an average of $1.69 dollars per year, while at the end of 2023 active users had grown to 62M and each one spent an average of $4.91 dollars.

The takeaway is that the company is growing in terms of user adoption and demonstrating that its platform is popular, but it's also getting users to spend more on memberships/hardware/services. Currently Snapchat has 800 million monthly users (but it's a social network so it's not entirely comparable) and it is estimated that Google Maps has 1,000 million monthly active users, showing that there's also potential to grow in this aspect considering the barely 62 million of Life360.

Life360's gross margins are quite high considering the software nature of the company, even considering hardware (which is typically low margin) the company has maintained an average gross margin of 75% and in the first two quarters of fiscal 2024 It has been 76%.

I don't think there's much room for expansion but I do think it can maintain this level for many years.

Another key note is that the company has already reached breakeven in EBITDA during Q2 2024, which sets the path for operating leverage due to the low costs of operating the business. The objective mentioned in the investor presentation is to reach margins of 25% EBITDA, which I consider really achievable.

Theoretically, the company is cash flow positive and in 2023 it generated $26 million of free cash flow, but this is because the stock-based compentsation was $39 million which is not a real cash flow, therefore I don't consider it has reached cash flow breakeven (although it's very close to achieving it).

Valuation

As we saw throughout the article, the company has opportunities to grow both in monthly active users and in revenue per user. For my estimate, users would increase about 20% annually until reaching 160 million and revenue per user would grow 15% annually until reaching almost $10 dollars per user. This coincides with the goal of reaching 150M active users and $1 billion in revenue.

This would result in sales growth of 35% annually, assuming the target EBITDA margins of 25% are reached (achievable considering the business is mostly software) and the company trades at a valuation multiple of 25 times EV/EBITDA, performance would be negative still. That is, it would be very overvalued currently from my point of view.

To obtain a return of 14% annually in the next five years, the company would have to grow revenue 50% annually with margins of 25% and a multiple of 30 times EV/EBITDA. It's not the most favorable situation to start since it would be priced to perfection. I'm not saying it's impossible, but it does seem like the safety margin is zero.

Risks

Beyond valuation, a big risk is that the company competes with many powerful brands. In localization there's Snapchat, which has the advantage of also being a social network, in devices there's Apple as I mentioned and of course there are many other apps to track family members because are very easy to develop.

Given this, the company must develop a powerful value proposition and brand power that makes users not want to change it, which remains to be proven and it's not guaranteed. If we add to this the demanding valuation, we have a formula for an IPO that may not necessarily be a failed one, but could be sideways for a while, as is the case of Air Bnb: A great company with growth but that went public at an expensive price and has been sideways since December 2020 (IPO date).

The Bottom Line

I love the company as a user, the freemium model allows you to develop affinity with the brand and a certain loyalty, but I'm still not entirely sure that the day will come when I decide to pay a subscription for Life360 when there are other ways to know the location of my loved ones, so the ad model is the income stream I'm most excited about going forward, along with elder/pet/child care which would have more value to users, but it's not yet fully developed.

Coupled with a rich valuation, I don't think now is the best time to invest in this company, so it would be best to give it a few years to mature and wait until it can be acquired at a reasonable valuation.
